query: do uterine myomas grow during pregnancy

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Uterus -- Pathophysiology -- Leiomyoma
retrieved_docs.content: As fibroids are estrogen-dependent, they are found primarily in pre-menopausal women between the ages of 20 and 40. They tend to grow in size during periods of increased estrogen (eg, pregnancy) and shrink when menopause occurs. Fibroids are typically asymptomatic unless they are unusually large or numerous, which can cause AUB and a feeling of "fullness" within the pelvis. Both intramural and submucosal fibroids are associated with infertility and carry a higher risk of miscarriage. If the fibroids are large or numerous, this can lead to fetal malpresentation, preterm labor, and/or postpartum hemorrhage.

retrieved_docs.title: Uterine Leiomyomata -- Treatment / Management -- Myomectomy for fertility improvement
retrieved_docs.content: The negative impact of intramural leiomyomata on fertility outcomes has also been demonstrated in multiple studies. In general, women with IM fibroids have lower rates of implantation, clinical pregnancy, and ongoing pregnancy/live birth. [115] [116] One of the largest prospective cohort studies investigating the success of in vitro fertilization (IVF) in women with and without leiomyomata showed that in women with intramural leiomyomata ≤5 cm (mean size 2.3 cm), the clinical pregnancy rate per embryo transfer was 23.3% compared to 34.1% in women without leiomyomata. [119]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Abdomen and Pelvis: Uterus -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Leiomyoma, or fibroid, is the most common benign tumor in women, often presenting as multiple discrete tumors within the uterine cavity. Leiomyoma is more prevalent in African-American women and is an estrogen-sensitive smooth muscle tumor. Although benign, the lesions have a slightly elevated risk of transforming into malignant leiomyosarcoma. These tumors tend to grow during pregnancy and shrink after menopause. Leiomyomas may cause abnormal uterine bleeding, miscarriages, and severe bleeding, leading to iron deficiency anemia in younger women. [13] The lesions often distort the shape of the uterus and can compress the rectum, resulting in constipation. Oral medications are typically ineffective, and surgical interventions, such as hysterectomy or myomectomy, are considered the gold standard for treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Fertilization -- Testing
retrieved_docs.content: Infertility is a common problem encountered in the medical community. If a female patient cannot become pregnant after one year of unprotected intercourse performed regularly, an evaluation is in order. An important consideration is the patient’s body mass index. If a patient is overweight, weight loss may improve her chances of becoming pregnant. [11] It is essential to consider labs in patients who are unable to become pregnant because abnormalities of the thyroid gland or androgen excess may indicate an endocrinopathy. A commonly encountered endocrinopathy that causes issues with fertility is polycystic ovarian syndrome. In PCOS, testosterone increases, which interferes with egg maturation. [12] A physical exam is also useful in evaluating infertility. Masses or tenderness in the adnexae or the pouch of Douglas is consistent with endometriosis or chronic pelvic inflammatory disease. Furthermore, structural abnormalities may suggest an infection, leiomyoma, malignancy, or Mullerian anomaly. In women who are not suspected to be ovulating, it is crucial to do a thorough hormone analysis to investigate if ovulation is occurring. A mid-luteal progesterone level should be tested one week before expected menstruation. To show proof of ovulation, a progesterone level greater than 3 ng/mL is the expectation. Another useful hormone to evaluate female fertility is the anti-Mullerian hormone. [13] This hormone is in the TGF-beta family and expresses itself by small early antral follicles. These levels reflect the size of the primordial follicle pool and are a good indicator of ovarian function.

retrieved_docs.title: Fertility Sparing Management in Uterine Fibroids -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The exact initiating factor of fibroid formation remains unclear, though prevailing theories highlight reproductive hormones, inherent abnormalities of the myometrium, and predisposing genetic variations as contributing factors. [11] Epidemiological studies also underscore the role of estrogen and progesterone since an increased incidence of fibroids has been found in premenopausal women, have undergone early menarche or DES exposure, or have an elevated BMI.  Perhaps the most striking evidence supporting this relationship is the tendency of fibroids to grow during early pregnancy and regress during the postpartum period or with the use of hormone-suppressing GnRH analogs.  Molecular studies help clarify this link. Myometrial cells with high levels of estrogen and progesterone receptors have been shown to stimulate the growth of adjacent fibroid stem cells in a paracrine fashion. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Uterine Leiomyomata -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Uterine fibroids are benign monoclonal tumors originating from smooth muscle cells within the uterus. [8] The exact initiating factor of fibroid formation remains unclear. However, prevailing theories highlight the role of reproductive hormones, inherent abnormalities of the myometrium, and predisposing genetic variations that affect cell signaling pathways. [9] [10] [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Uterine Leiomyomata -- Treatment / Management -- Magnetic resonance-guided focused ultrasound surgery
retrieved_docs.content: A study performed by Funaki et al looking at 91 women over 24 months who underwent MRgFUS demonstrated a mean reduction in leiomyoma  volume of 36.5% at 6 months and 39.5% at 24 months. [130] Another study showed reductions in leiomyoma volume of 86%, 93%, and 88% at 3, 6, and 12 months, respectively. [131] In patients with primarily intramural myomas >3 cm and uteri size <24 weeks gestation, MRgFUS decreased the percentage of patients reporting excessive bleeding from 69% to 31% and relieved pressure symptoms in 49% of patients. [132]

retrieved_docs.title: Uterine Leiomyomata -- History and Physical -- Clinical History
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with leiomyomata may also present with infertility complaints. Adverse effects of leiomyomas on fertility are largely dependent on their location and ability to distort normal uterine and adnexal anatomy, thereby preventing normal gamete transport, embryo implantation, and the maintenance of early pregnancy. [44] Studies have shown evidence suggesting uterine leiomyomata can alter endometrial receptivity, impair local blood flow, and lead to chronic inflammation that creates a hostile environment for successful intrauterine pregnancy. [45] [44]

retrieved_docs.title: Fertility Sparing Management in Uterine Fibroids -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Fibroids develop as inappropriate yet benign growths of monoclonal uterine cells, specifically smooth muscle cells and fibroblasts. [3] Though pathogenesis typically occurs in the myometrium, fibroids can extend superficially from the uterine serosa from the pelvis into the lower abdomen and, on occasion, into the upper abdomen as well. Alternatively. they can arise from the myometrium and delve into the uterine cavity.

retrieved_docs.title: Uterine Leiomyomata -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Some notable risk factors for fibroid recurrence after surgical or procedural treatment include age less than 35.5 years at treatment, 2 or fewer fibroids at the time of surgery, size of the uterus less than 13 weeks gestation, and no childbearing after surgery. [148] At 8 years postoperation, ABD-M has a 63.4% risk of fibroid recurrence compared to 76.2% after LSC-M. [111] Reintervention rates range from 19% to 38% after UAE and are as high as 54% after MRgFUS. [124] [88]

retrieved_docs.title: Fertility Sparing Management in Uterine Fibroids -- Treatment / Management -- Magnetic Resonance–guided Focused Ultrasound Surgery (MRgFUS)
retrieved_docs.content: A study performed by Funaki et al. looking at 91 women over 24 months who underwent MRgFUS demonstrated a mean reduction in the volume of 36.5% at six months and 39.5% at 24 months. [95] Another study looking at 12-month follow-up showed an 86%, 93%, and 88% at 3, 6, and 12 months, respectively. [96] In patients with primarily intramural myomas >3cm and uteri size < 24 weeks of gestation, MRgFUS decreased the percentage of patients reporting excessive bleeding from 69 to 31% and relieving pressure symptoms in 49% of patients. [97] There is limited research regarding reproductive outcomes after MRgFUS, with most data coming from a few case reports and small case series.  Existing data is reassuring, with one case series describing 54 pregnancies in 51 women after MRgFUS. [98] More studies are needed to determine whether MRgFUS should be recommended for patients seeking future fertility.

retrieved_docs.title: Sonography Female Pelvic Pathology Assessment, Protocols, and Interpretation  -- Indications -- Abdominal Mass and Pelvic Mass
retrieved_docs.content: Ascites and ovarian malignancy frequently present primarily with complaints of increased abdominal girth, abdominal distension, and/or palpable pelvic mass. Large subserosal broad ligament leiomyoma, multiple uterine leiomyomas, adenomyosis with more than 12-week pregnant uterine size, molar pregnancy, rudimentary horn ectopic pregnancy, hematometra, pyometra, choriocarcinoma, ovarian and tubo ovarian mass present as pelvic mass frequently extending up to abdominal cavity. All these mentioned pelvic pathologies can be screened by pelvic sonography.

retrieved_docs.title: Breech Presentation -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Mullerian anomalies: Septate uterus, bicornuate uterus, and didelphys uterus Placentation: Placenta previa as the placenta occupies the inferior portion of the uterine cavity. Therefore, the presenting part cannot engage Uterine leiomyoma: Larger myomas are mainly located in the lower uterine segment, often intramural or submucosal, and prevent engagement of the presenting part. Prematurity Aneuploidies and fetal neuromuscular disorders commonly cause hypotonia of the fetus, inability to move effectively Congenital anomalies:  Fetal sacrococcygeal teratoma, fetal thyroid goiter Polyhydramnios: The fetus is often in an unstable lie, unable to engage Oligohydramnios: Fetus is unable to turn to the vertex due to lack of fluid Laxity of the maternal abdominal wall: The Uterus falls forward, and the fetus cannot engage in the pelvis.

retrieved_docs.title: Pregnancy Dating -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Fundal height measurement is a physical exam parameter that can be used to estimate gestational age. The distance from the uterine fundus to the pubic symphysis defines fundal height measurement. Measurement should be performed using a nonelastic tape measure, and the patient should have an empty bladder. The most common use for fundal height measurement is recording the trend of this measurement to screen for appropriate fetal growth throughout gestation. The usefulness of fundal height measurement in any circumstance has varied widely throughout the literature but can be helpful in resource-poor areas for an estimation of gestational age. The assumption with fundal height measurement is that the measurement in centimeters from the uterine fundus to pubic symphysis is equal to the patient's gestational age. Uterine fibroids, amniotic fluid abnormalities, increased maternal body mass index, and fetal growth abnormalities are some examples of circumstances that can alter the accuracy of fundal height measurement. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Adenomyosis -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The physical exam classically demonstrates a “boggy” enlarged uterus due to the combined effects of increased vascularization from ectopic endometrial tissue and smooth muscle proliferation. As compared to the enlarged leiomyomatous uterus, a tender uterus is more common. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Uterine Leiomyomata -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Additionally, uterine leiomyomata are hormonally sensitive tumors, and evidence has demonstrated that leiomyomas overexpress certain estrogen and progesterone receptors when compared to normal surrounding myometrium. [17] [18] Studies indicate that the ovarian steroids estradiol and progesterone promote the growth of leiomyomas, and myometrial cells with high levels of estrogen and progesterone receptors can stimulate the growth of adjacent uterine leiomyomata stem cells in a paracrine fashion. [19] Additionally, the size of uterine leiomyomata typically declines during the postpartum period, with the use of hormone-suppressing gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) analogs, and after menopause, when levels of those hormones fall. [20]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1740
retrieved_docs.content: ورم عضلي أملس رحمي, a uterine smooth muscle tumor, is classified as a benign neoplasm of the uterus and originates from the female internal genitalia structure. Clinically, this tumor has been associated with a congenital malformation observed in a previous pregnancy, with documented size dates, and with an uterine scar of unknown type. Recognizing these associations aids clinicians in evaluating risk factors, monitoring tumor growth, and planning management strategies for patients with uterine smooth muscle tumors.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1733
retrieved_docs.content: CONGENITAL MALFORMATION PREVIOUS PREGNANCY, Gynecologic factors, SIZE DATES, and UTERINE SCAR TYPE UNKNOWN are all clinically associated with ورم عضلي أملس رحمي (uterine smooth‑muscle tumor or leiomyoma). The presence of a congenital malformation related to a prior pregnancy may predispose to the development of uterine leiomyoma, while broader gynecologic considerations are also linked to this tumor. Information about the tumor’s size and the dates of its detection (SIZE DATES) provides important clinical context for assessing disease burden and progression. Additionally, an unknown type of uterine scar (UTERINE SCAR TYPE UNKNOWN) is associated with the occurrence of ورم عضلي أملس رحمي, suggesting that scar characteristics may influence tumor formation or presentation. Recognizing these interconnected factors can improve diagnostic evaluation, risk stratification, and individualized management of patients with uterine leiomyoma.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 1541
retrieved_docs.content: CONGENITAL MALFORMATION PREVIOUS PREGNANCY, Gynecologic factors, SIZE DATES, and UTERINE SCAR TYPE UNKNOWN are all clinically associated with ورم عضلي أملس رحمي (uterine smooth muscle tumor). The entity ورم عضلي أملس رحمي is mapped from أورام ليفية رحمية متضخمة (enlarged uterine fibroid tumors), indicating that the smooth‑muscle tumor represents a manifestation of these fibroid growths. Additionally, أورام ليفية رحمية متضخمة is categorized under the broader parent group UTERINE/ENDOMETRIAL DISORDERS. Together, these relationships highlight that uterine smooth muscle tumors are linked to various gynecologic and obstetric conditions—including congenital malformations from previous pregnancies and unknown uterine scar types—and are considered part of the spectrum of uterine and endometrial disorders.

Recognizing the links between prior congenital malformations of pregnancy, broader gynecologic conditions, and size‑related clinical observations with ورم عضلي أملس رحمي can aid clinicians in identifying patients at risk, guiding diagnostic evaluation, and informing appropriate management strategies for this common benign uterine tumor.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 1783
retrieved_docs.content: The congenital malformation from a previous pregnancy, the benign neoplasm of uterus, and the clinical parameter referred to as “SIZE DATES” are all clinically associated with a uterine smooth muscle tumor (ورم عضلي أملس رحمي). In this context, the benign neoplasm of uterus is classified as a subtype of the uterine smooth muscle tumor, indicating that such neoplasms are part of the broader category of smooth‑muscle growths within the uterus. The association of a prior congenital malformation during pregnancy with the uterine smooth muscle tumor suggests a possible link between developmental uterine abnormalities and the later development of these tumors. Additionally, the relationship between “SIZE DATES” and the uterine smooth muscle tumor highlights the importance of tumor size measurement and dating in the clinical evaluation and management of these lesions. Together, these connections underscore the relevance of developmental history, tumor classification, and size assessment in understanding and caring for patients with uterine smooth muscle tumors.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 1 -- Community 212
retrieved_docs.content: Abnormal uterine cervix morphology is classified as a form of شذوذ الرحم الخلقي (congenital uterine anomaly). Congenital abnormalities of the uterus, unspecified for episode of care, are described as having a broader relationship with شذوذ الرحم الخلقي, indicating that various structural uterine malformations fall under this congenital category. Both شذوذ الرحم الخلقي and ورم عضلي أملس رحمي (uterine smooth‑muscle tumor) are sub‑entities of the broader category Female internal genitalia structure, highlighting that they are specific conditions affecting the internal female reproductive organs. In the gynecologic domain, ورم عضلي أملس رحمي is a key associated condition, and several clinical factors—including CONGENITAL MALFORMATION PREVIOUS PREGNANCY, SIZE DATES, and UTERINE SCAR TYPE UNKNOWN—are each clinically associated with this smooth‑muscle tumor, suggesting that a history of uterine malformations, tumor size measurements, and unknown scar types may influence its presentation or management. ورم عضلي أملس رحمي also frequently co‑occurs with كيسة رحمية (uterine cyst), indicating a common concurrent pathology. The term أورام ليفية رحمية متضخمة (enlarged uterine fibroid tumors) is mapped to ورم عضلي أملس رحمي, and UTERINE/ENDOMETRIAL DISORDERS is identified as the parent category for أورام ليفية رحمية متضخمة, placing the smooth‑muscle tumor within the larger spectrum of uterine and endometrial disorders. Together, these relationships illustrate how congenital uterine anomalies, specific gynecologic tumors, associated clinical histories, and related cystic lesions are interconnected within female reproductive health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1619
retrieved_docs.content: The uterine smooth muscle tumor (ورم عضلي أملس رحمي) is linked to several clinical and anatomical factors. The size and dating characteristics of the lesion are clinically associated, indicating that tumor dimensions and temporal aspects are relevant to its presentation. An unknown type of uterine scar is clinically associated with the tumor, suggesting that prior uterine injury may contribute to its formation. Finally, the tumor originates from the female internal genitalia structure, indicating that the uterine smooth muscle is its parent tissue. This integrated view highlights the multifactorial relationships influencing uterine smooth muscle tumors.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1444
retrieved_docs.content: Congenital malformation in a previous pregnancy, the measurement referred to as size dates, and an unknown type of uterine scar are each clinically associated with both spontaneous abortion (إجهاض تلقائي) and uterine leiomyoma (ورم عضلي أملس رحمي). These associations suggest that a history of congenital malformations, certain uterine measurements, and the presence of an indeterminate uterine scar may increase the risk of both miscarriage and the development of smooth‑muscle uterine tumors. In contrast, the progestogen dydrogesterone may help prevent spontaneous abortion, indicating a potential therapeutic role for this medication in patients at risk for miscarriage. Recognizing these interrelationships can guide clinicians in risk assessment, monitoring, and preventive strategies for women with these reproductive health concerns.
